coredata.dc:title: Research on peak power test method for Lithium Ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Xiaofeng
Sun, Bingxiang
Qi, Hongfeng
Shen, Xiaobo
Su, Xiaojia
coredata.dc:description: The peak power of the battery (SOP) is an important parameter index for electric vehicle to improve the efficiency of battery utilization and ensure the safety of the system in the maximum limit. The estimation and prediction of SOP is based on a large number of test data at different temperature, different SOC and different time scales. It is important to improve SOP test efficiency under the premise of guaranteeing accuracy, which is also significant for BMS algorithm design and development investment reduction. In this paper, with 2.75Ah ternary Li-ion battery as the research object, the test efficiency and accuracy of the current peak power test methods (HPPC, JEVS and constant current charge and discharge ) are compared at different temperatures, through optimizing the JEVS, the test results meet the requirements at different time scales on the basis of high efficiency test, and the optimized JEVS method is verified. The experimental results indicate that the optimized JEVS can accurately estimate the peak power of the battery at different temperatures and greatly shorten the test time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium Ion
peak power
HPPC
optimized JEVS
constant current charge
discharge

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids confeditor contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj suppl tomb vol volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 152 ( 8) 55 555 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the CUE2018-Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems. 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.09.209 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.09.209 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the CUE2018-Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems. 1876-6102 CUE2018-Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Research on peak power test method for Lithium Ion battery Xiaofeng Shena, Bingxiang Suna, Hongfeng Qib, Xiaobo Shenc, Xiaojia Suaä¸€ aNational Active Distribution Network Technology Research Center, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing, 100044, China bCRRC Insdustrial Institute Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100070, China cHao Hua Engineering Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100143, China Abstract The peak power of the battery (SOP) is an important parameter index for electric vehicle to improve the efficiency of battery utilization and ensure the safety of the system in the maximum limit. The estimation and prediction of SOP is based on a large number of test data at different temperature, different SOC and different time scales. It is important to improve SOP test efficiency under the premise of guaranteeing accuracy, which is also significant for BMS algorithm design and development investment reduction. In this paper, with 2.75Ah ternary Li-ion battery as the research object, the test efficiency and accuracy of the current peak power test methods (HPPC, JEVS and constant current charge and discharge ) are compared at different temperatures, through optimizing the JEVS, the test results meet the requirements at different time scales on the basis of high efficiency test, and the optimized JEVS method is verified. The experimental results indicate that the optimized JEVS can accurately estimate the peak power of the battery at different temperatures and greatly shorten the test time. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Sy posium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywo d : Lithium Ion; pe k power; HPPC; optimized JEVS; constant current charge and discharge 1. Introduction In recent years, with the rapid development of energy and technology, hybrid electric vehicles have become the key direction of traffic development in various countries in the future [1]. Since lithium battery has advantages of high energy density, long lifespan and low cost, it is widely used in the battery management system (BMS) of electric vehicles [2]. It is essential to accurately test the battery peak power (SOP) in the BMS [3,4], the battery may * C rresponding author. Tel.: +86-186-10675580. E-mail address: 16121510@bjtu.edu.cn; qihongfeng@crrcgc.cc Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Co yright Â© 2018 Els vier Ltd. All rig ts reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Research on peak power test method for Lithium Ion battery Xiaofeng Shena, Bingxiang Suna, Hongfeng Qib, Xiaobo Shenc, Xiaojia Suaä¸€ aNational Active Distribution Network Technology Research Center, Be jing Jiaotong U iversity, Beijing, 100044, China bCRRC Insdustrial Institute Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100070, China cHao Hua Engineering Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100143, China Abstract The peak power of the battery (SOP) i an important parameter index for electric vehicle to improve the efficiency of battery utilization and ensure the safety of the system in the maximum limit. Th esti ation and prediction of SOP is based on a large numbe of test data at diff rent temperature, different SOC and differe t time scales. It is important to improv SOP t st efficiency un er the premise of guaranteeing accuracy, which is also significant for BMS algorithm design and development inve m t reduction. In this paper, with 2.75Ah ternary Li-i b ttery the research object, the test efficie cy an ccuracy of the current pe k power test methods (HPPC, JEVS and constant current charge and discharge ) are compar d at diff rent temp ratures, through optimizing th JEVS, t e test res lts meet th requir ment at different time scal s on the basis of high efficienc test, d the optimiz d JEVS method is v ified. The experimental results indicate that the optimized JEVS can accurately e timate the peak power of the battery at different temperatures and greatly shorten the t st time. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection a d p er-review un r res onsibility of the sci ntific committee of Applied Ene gy Symposium n Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy syste s, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithium Ion; peak power; HPPC; op imized JEVS; constant current charge and di cha ge 1. Introduction In recent years, with the rapid development of energy and technology, hybrid electric vehicles have become the key direction of traffic development in various countries in the future [1]. Since lithium battery has advantages of high energy density, long lifespan and low cost, it is widely used in the battery management system (BMS) of electric vehicles [2]. It is essential to accurately test the battery peak power (SOP) in the BMS [3,4], the battery may * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86-186-10675580. E-mail address: 16121510@bjtu.edu.cn; qi o feng@crrcgc.cc 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 be overcharged or overdischarged by exceeding the designed current or voltage limit during charge or discharge if the SOP is unknown [5]. The method of multistage pulsed discharge is used to estimate SOP [6], but all SOC can not be estimated. Document [7] proposes a method which can accurately estimate the SOP, and points out that the factors affecting SOP mainly include temperature, SOC and time scale, but constant power test needs repeated random experiments and a lot of time. Therefore, it is necessary to find a method which not only can efficiently test battery peak power, but also can meet different time scaleâ€™s needs, this method can effectively provide input power and output power with different time scales for BMS. Thus, it is of great significance to accurately test the peak power of battery at different temperatures, different SOC and different time scales. In this paper, the research object is 2.75Ah lithium ion battery. Peak current can be directly characterized by the peak power, so we use HPPC, optimized JEVS and constant current charge/discharge to test the battery peak current between 5%SOC and 95%SOC at different duration in 10â„ƒ, 25â„ƒ and 45â„ƒ. The applicability of the optimized JEVS test method in the study of the peak power test of lithium ion batteries is analyzed based on the experimental results of different test methods. 2. Test methods for peak power 2.1. HPPC test According to the Freedom CAR Battery Test Manual [8], 1C charge for 10s, reset 40s, 4C/3 discharge 10s. Using the formula (1) to calculate the charge ohmic internal resistance Rch and the formula (2) to calculate the discharge ohmic internal resistance Rdis corresponding to different SOC. The 0U , 1U , 2U and 3U is showed in Fig. 1(a). chi UU R 10 ch ï€­ï€½ (1) dis d 32 i UU R is ï€­ï€½ (2) ch OCV R UU I ï€­ï€½ maxch (3) dis OCV R UU I min dis ï€­ï€½ (4) Fig. 1. HPPC test: (a) Voltage profile of HPPC (20%SOC), (b) Peak charge and discharge current of HPPC Using the Formula (3) and (4) to calculate the peak charge/discharge current corresponding to different SOC. Umax and Umin is the battery charge and discharge cut-off voltage, while the UOCV is the battery open circuit voltage. The result of HPPC test is showed in Fig. 1(b), the peak current increases first and then decreases with increasing SOC. Xiaofeng Shen et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 550â€“555 551 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Research on peak power test method for Lithium Ion battery Xiaofeng Shena, Bingxiang Suna, Hongfeng Qib, Xiaobo Shenc, Xiaojia Suaä¸€ aNational Active Distribution Network Technology Research Center, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing, 100044, China bCRRC Insdustrial Institute Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100070, China cHao Hua Engineering Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100143, China Abstract The peak power of the battery (SOP) is an important parameter index for electric vehicle to improve the efficiency of battery utilization and ensure the safety of the system in the maximum limit. The estimation and prediction of SOP is based on a large number of test data at different temperature, different SOC and different time scales. It is important to improve SOP test efficiency under the premise of guaranteeing accuracy, which is also significant for BMS algorithm design and development investment reduction. In this paper, with 2.75Ah ternary Li-ion battery as the research object, the test efficiency and accuracy of the current peak power test methods (HPPC, JEVS and constant current charge and discharge ) are compared at different temperatures, through optimizing the JEVS, the test results meet the requirements at different time scales on the basis of high efficiency test, and the optimized JEVS method is verified. The experimental results indicate that the optimized JEVS can accurately estimate the peak power of the battery at different temperatures and greatly shorten the test time. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithium Ion; peak power; HPPC; optimized JEVS; constant current charge and discharge 1. Introduction In recent years, with the rapid development of energy and technology, hybrid electric vehicles have become the key direction of traffic development in various countries in the future [1]. Since lithium battery has advantages of high energy density, long lifespan and low cost, it is widely used in the battery management system (BMS) of electric vehicles [2]. It is essential to accurately test the battery peak power (SOP) in the BMS [3,4], the battery may * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86-186-10675580. E-mail address: 16121510@bjtu.edu.cn; qihongfeng@crrcgc.cc Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Co yright Â© 2018 Els vier Ltd. All rig ts reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018, 5â€“7 June 2018, Shanghai, China Research on peak power test method for Lithium Ion battery Xiaofeng Shena, Bingxiang Suna, Hongfeng Qib, Xiaobo Shenc, Xiaojia Suaä¸€ aNational Active Distribution Network Technology Research Center, Be jing Jiaotong U iversity, Beijing, 100044, China bCRRC Insdustrial Institute Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100070, China cHao Hua Engineering Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100143, China Abstract The peak power of the battery (SOP) i an important parameter index for electric vehicle to improve the efficiency of battery utilization and ensure the safety of the system in the maximum limit. Th esti ation and prediction of SOP is based on a large number of test data at diff rent temperature, different SOC and differe t time scales. It is important to improv SOP t st efficiency un er the premise of guaranteeing accuracy, which is also significant for BMS algorithm design and development investm t reduction. In this paper, with 2.75Ah ternary Li-i b ttery as the research object, the test efficiency an ccuracy of the current peak power test methods (HPPC, JEVS and constant current charge and discharge ) are compar d at different temp ratures, through optimizing the JEVS, t e test results meet th requir ments at different time scal s on the basis of high efficienc test, nd the optimiz d JEVS method is v ified. The experimental results indicate that the optimized JEVS can accurately estimate the peak power of the battery at different temperatures and greatly shorten the test time. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of Applied Energy Symposium and Forum 2018: Low carbon cities and urban energy systems, CUE2018. Keywords: Lithium Ion; peak power; HPPC; optimized JEVS; constant current charge and discharge 1. Introduction In recent years, with the rapid development of energy and technology, hybrid electric vehicles have become the key direction of traffic development in various countries in the future [1]. Since lithium battery has advantages of high energy density, long lifespan and low cost, it is widely used in the battery management system (BMS) of electric vehicles [2]. It is essential to accurately test the battery peak power (SOP) in the BMS [3,4], the battery may * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86-186-10675580. E-mail address: 16121510@bjtu.edu.cn; qihongfeng@crrcgc.cc 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 be overcharged or overdischarged by exceeding the designed current or voltage limit during charge or discharge if the SOP is unknown [5]. The method of multistage pulsed discharge is used to estimate SOP [6], but all SOC can not be estimated. Document [7] proposes a method which can accurately estimate the SOP, and points out that the factors affecting SOP mainly include temperature, SOC and time scale, but constant power test needs repeated random experiments and a lot of time. Therefore, it is necessary to find a method which not only can efficiently test battery peak power, but also can meet different time scaleâ€™s needs, this method can effectively provide input power and output power with different time scales for BMS. Thus, it is of great significance to accurately test the peak power of battery at different temperatures, different SOC and different time scales. In this paper, the research object is 2.75Ah lithium ion battery. Peak current can be directly characterized by the peak power, so we use HPPC, optimized JEVS and constant current charge/discharge to test the battery peak current between 5%SOC and 95%SOC at different duration in 10â„ƒ, 25â„ƒ and 45â„ƒ. The applicability of the optimized JEVS test method in the study of the peak power test of lithium ion batteries is analyzed based on the experimental results of different test methods. 2. Test methods for peak power 2.1. HPPC test According to the Freedom CAR Battery Test Manual [8], 1C charge for 10s, reset 40s, 4C/3 discharge 10s. Using the formula (1) to calculate the charge ohmic internal resistance Rch and the formula (2) to calculate the discharge ohmic internal resistance Rdis corresponding to different SOC. The 0U , 1U , 2U and 3U is showed in Fig. 1(a). chi UU R 10 ch ï€­ï€½ (1) dis d 32 i UU R is ï€­ï€½ (2) ch OCV R UU I ï€­ï€½ maxch (3) dis OCV R UU I min dis ï€­ï€½ (4) Fig. 1. HPPC test: (a) Voltage profile of HPPC (20%SOC), (b) Peak charge and discharge current of HPPC Using the Formula (3) and (4) to calculate the peak charge/discharge current corresponding to different SOC. Umax and Umin is the battery charge and discharge cut-off voltage, while the UOCV is the battery open circuit voltage. The result of HPPC test is showed in Fig. 1(b), the peak current increases first and then decreases with increasing SOC. 552 Xiaofeng Shen et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 550â€“555 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 2.2. Optimized JEVS test The traditional JEVS test [9] is used to charge or discharge 10s with different currents at the same SOC, reset 1h. But the peak charging current is far less than the discharging current at high SOC, if still charge with the same current, will exceed the cut-off voltage during charging. So need to improve the JEVS test to get the peak discharge current at high SOC. Taking 80%SOC as an example in Fig. 2(a), alternately charging or discharging for 10 seconds with a small rate pulse. Then charge the same discharge capacity at each discharge so that we can guarantee battery SOC constant and accurately measures peak discharge current, the part of experimental test procedure of optimized JEVS is shown in Table 1. Recorded the corresponding voltage at the end of each charging or discharging. Fitting them with a straight line and the characteristic curve of the charging or discharging direction of the SOC point is obtained. Extend the current-voltage curve to find the current corresponding to the charge or discharge cut-off voltage. the current is the peak charging or discharging current at that SOC, which is showed in Figure Fig. 2(b). Table 1. Partially optimized JEVS experimental test procedure (SOC80%). Process definition Main parameter Restrictive conditions Recording condition Discharge 2C 10s 10ms Rest 1h 1s Charge 0.5C 0.0153Ah 10ms Rest 1h 1s Discharge 3C 10s 10ms Rest 1h 1s Charge 0.5C 0.0229Ah 10ms Rest 1h 1s Discharge 4C 10s 10ms Rest 1h 1s Charge 0.5C 0.0305Ah 10ms Rest 1h 1s Discharge 6C 10s 10ms Rest 1h 1s Charge 0.5C 0.0458Ah 10ms Rest 1h 1s Fig. 2. Optimized JEVS test: (a) Current and voltage profiles of optimized JEVS, (b) Peak charge and discharge current of optimized JEVS 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 2.3. Constant current charge and discharge test Take the constant current charging test as an example, which is showed in Fig. 3. Fig. 3. Current and voltage profiles of constant current charge test Randomly select a charging current I at one point SOC. Charging until battery voltage reaches the cut-off voltage (4.2V) and record the test time t. Repeat the experiment more than five times by changing the size of the current I to make the time t gradually approach 10 seconds. Then fit the time t to current I, and the Imax corresponding to 10s is the peak charge current of the battery. The constant current discharge test is same as this, and we can adjust battery SOC to get peak current in different SOC . 3. Experimental results and analysis of SOP Randomly selected three batteries numbered #17, #18, and #19, the results of three batteries with constant current charge/discharge test are shown in Fig.4, it can be seen from Fig.4 that peak discharge current increases along with SOC increase, but peak charge current decreases along with SOC increase, the peak discharge current is greater than the peak charge current between 20%SOC and 95%SOC. Fig. 4. Constant current charge/discharge test for peak current of different batteries The peak current of different batteries is almost same, So select one of the experimental results to verify HPPC and optimized JEVS, the results of the three methods are shown in Fig.5(a). Xiaofeng Shen et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 550â€“555 553 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 2.2. Optimized JEVS test The traditional JEVS test [9] is used to charge or discharge 10s with different currents at the same SOC, reset 1h. But the peak charging current is far less than the discharging current at high SOC, if still charge with the same current, will exceed the cut-off voltage during charging. So need to improve the JEVS test to get the peak discharge current at high SOC. Taking 80%SOC as an example in Fig. 2(a), alternately charging or discharging for 10 seconds with a small rate pulse. Then charge the same discharge capacity at each discharge so that we can guarantee battery SOC constant and accurately measures peak discharge current, the part of experimental test procedure of optimized JEVS is shown in Table 1. Recorded the corresponding voltage at the end of each charging or discharging. Fitting them with a straight line and the characteristic curve of the charging or discharging direction of the SOC point is obtained. Extend the current-voltage curve to find the current corresponding to the charge or discharge cut-off voltage. the current is the peak charging or discharging current at that SOC, which is showed in Figure Fig. 2(b). Table 1. Partially optimized JEVS experimental test procedure (SOC80%). Process definition Main parameter Restrictive conditions Recording condition Discharge 2C 10s 10ms Rest 1h 1s Charge 0.5C 0.0153Ah 10ms Rest 1h 1s Discharge 3C 10s 10ms Rest 1h 1s Charge 0.5C 0.0229Ah 10ms Rest 1h 1s Discharge 4C 10s 10ms Rest 1h 1s Charge 0.5C 0.0305Ah 10ms Rest 1h 1s Discharge 6C 10s 10ms Rest 1h 1s Charge 0.5C 0.0458Ah 10ms Rest 1h 1s Fig. 2. Optimized JEVS test: (a) Current and voltage profiles of optimized JEVS, (b) Peak charge and discharge current of optimized JEVS 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 2.3. Constant current charge and discharge test Take the constant current charging test as an example, which is showed in Fig. 3. Fig. 3. Current and voltage profiles of constant current charge test Randomly select a charging current I at one point SOC. Charging until battery voltage reaches the cut-off voltage (4.2V) and record the test time t. Repeat the experiment more than five times by changing the size of the current I to make the time t gradually approach 10 seconds. Then fit the time t to current I, and the Imax corresponding to 10s is the peak charge current of the battery. The constant current discharge test is same as this, and we can adjust battery SOC to get peak current in different SOC . 3. Experimental results and analysis of SOP Randomly selected three batteries numbered #17, #18, and #19, the results of three batteries with constant current charge/discharge test are shown in Fig.4, it can be seen from Fig.4 that peak discharge current increases along with SOC increase, but peak charge current decreases along with SOC increase, the peak discharge current is greater than the peak charge current between 20%SOC and 95%SOC. Fig. 4. Constant current charge/discharge test for peak current of different batteries The peak current of different batteries is almost same, So select one of the experimental results to verify HPPC and optimized JEVS, the results of the three methods are shown in Fig.5(a). 554 Xiaofeng Shen et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 550â€“555 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 Fig. 5. Constant current charge/discharge, optimized JEVS and HPPC: (a) Peak current of three tests, (b) Relative error of JEVS and HPPC HPPC test error is large at the low SOC during the charge process, and the error is large at the high and low SOC during the discharge process. The charge current at the low SOC will exceed the voltage limit if the charge current is too high, so the charge current estimated by the HPPC test at the low SOC is smaller. Similarly, the estimated discharge current is larger in the low SOC, and the estimated discharge current is smaller in the high SOC. Therefore, only using HPPC test results to estimate SOP may lead to overcharge or overdischarge, and the HPPC test does not set current limits, the charging current may exceed the battery limit design and reduce the battery life [10]. It can be seen from Fig.5(b) that the error of JEVS test is less than 10% during the charge process, the error is large at the low SOC but the error is less than 10% between 10%SOC and 95%SOC during the discharge process, because the discharge resistance is far larger than the charge resistance at the low SOC. To sum up, HPPC test is low precision and only a single low rate pulse is considered, is a special kind of JEVS test. However the optimized JEVS test can not only estimate the peak current, but also can eliminate the error caused by the traditional JEVS test, because the optimized JEVS through increasing the discharge pulse test to avoid the error caused by the nonlinear relationship between the polarization internal resistance and the discharge current. so the optimized JEVS test is used to accurately estimate the 10s peak power of different SOC under 25â„ƒ. Fig. 6. Constant current charge/discharge, optimized JEVS: (a) Peak current of diffenrent temperature (50%SOC), (b) Peak current of different duration (25â„ƒ,50%SOC) 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 In order to verify whether the optimized JEVS test is also suitable for battery power testing at high and low temperatures, we need to compare the optimized JEVS test with the constant current charge/discharge test at different temperatures. The test peak current in 10â„ƒ, 25â„ƒ and 45â„ƒ (50%SOC) is shown in Fig.6(a), the results show that the error of the peak current estimated by JEVS is still less than 10% in 10â„ƒ, 25â„ƒ and 45â„ƒ (50%SOC), so the optimized JEVS test is suitable for estimating the 10s peak power at different temperatures. Battery electric vehicles generally select 30s as the test pulse width, the peak power duration of the Hybrid Vehicles will not exceed 60s, so BMSs are required to provide real-time peak input power and peak output power with different durations in actual operation of electric vehicles. Therefore, it is necessary to compare constant current charge/discharge test with optimized JEVS test at different time scales, the test results are shown in Fig.6(b). The results show that the error of optimized JEVS test is still within 10% under different time scales in 25â„ƒ (SOC=0.5). Based on the above analysis, under the premise that the error is less than 10%, the optimized JEVS test can efficiently estimate the battery peak power at different temperatures, different SOC and different time scales. 4. Conclusion In this study, an optimized JEVS power test is presented, and three power testing methods are compared, the constant current charge and discharge test results are used as benchmark data. The optimized JEVS test can effectively estimate the battery peak current at different SOC compared with the HPPC test in 25â„ƒ . At the same time, in order to meet the actual operation demand of electric vehicles, comparative analysis of different temperatures and time scales are introduced. The results show that the optimized JEVS test can estimate the battery peak power at different temperatures, different SOC and different time scales accurately and quickly. The conclusion is important for preventing battery overcharge or overdischarge and improving battery safety. Furthermore, the optimized JEVS method can improve the estimation accuracy of peak power, which also provide the basis for improving the battery utilization and reducing the operating cost of vehicles. Acknowledgements This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China (Grant Number 2017YFB1201005). References [1] B.O. Varga, Electric vehicles, primary energy sources and CO2 emissions:Romanian case study, Energy 49 (2013) 51â€“72. [2] L. Saw, Y. Ye, A. Tay, Electro-thermal analysis and integration issues of lithiumion battery for electric vehicles, Appl. Energy 131 (2014) 97-107. [3] Lu L, Han X, Li J, Hua J, Ouyang M. A review on the key issues for lithium-ion battery management in electric vehicles. J Power Sources 2013;226:272-88. [4] Giuliano MR, Advani SG, Prasad AK. Thermal analysis and management of lithiumetitanate batteries. J Power Sources 2011;196(15):6517- 24 [5] J. C. Jiang, C. P. Zhang, The prediction of battery pack peak power, Fundamentals and Applications of Lithium-ion Batteries in Electric Drive Vehicles, John Wiley & Sons Singapore Pte. Ltd, Singapore. 2015, pp. 103â€“112. [6] Li Fang, Yang Yifu, Yang Lihua. HEV study the output power of the battery using[J]. power technology, 2009 (3):210-212 [7] Cui Jintao, Ceng Qingdong, Wang Tianli. Study on the peak power test method of lead-acid battery[J]. battery, 2016 (1): 15-17 [8] Idaho National Engineering &Environmental Laboratory ( INEEL). Freedom CAR Battery Test Manual for Power-Assist Hybrid Electric Vehicles. DOE/ID-11069, 2003, PDF version. [9] Japan electric vehicle society. Test method of input and out put power density of nickel-hydride battery for Hybrid Electric vehicles[S]. Japan: 2003. [10] Wu-Jie, Jiang, Ning, Zhang, Peng-Cheng, Li, Nan, Chen. A Temperature-Based Peak Power Capability Estimation Method for Lithium-Ion Batteries[J]. Procedia Engineering, 2017, (187): 249-256 Xiaofeng Shen et al. / Energy Procedia 152 (2018) 550â€“555 555 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 Fig. 5. Constant current charge/discharge, optimized JEVS and HPPC: (a) Peak current of three tests, (b) Relative error of JEVS and HPPC HPPC test error is large at the low SOC during the charge process, and the error is large at the high and low SOC during the discharge process. The charge current at the low SOC will exceed the voltage limit if the charge current is too high, so the charge current estimated by the HPPC test at the low SOC is smaller. Similarly, the estimated discharge current is larger in the low SOC, and the estimated discharge current is smaller in the high SOC. Therefore, only using HPPC test results to estimate SOP may lead to overcharge or overdischarge, and the HPPC test does not set current limits, the charging current may exceed the battery limit design and reduce the battery life [10]. It can be seen from Fig.5(b) that the error of JEVS test is less than 10% during the charge process, the error is large at the low SOC but the error is less than 10% between 10%SOC and 95%SOC during the discharge process, because the discharge resistance is far larger than the charge resistance at the low SOC. To sum up, HPPC test is low precision and only a single low rate pulse is considered, is a special kind of JEVS test. However the optimized JEVS test can not only estimate the peak current, but also can eliminate the error caused by the traditional JEVS test, because the optimized JEVS through increasing the discharge pulse test to avoid the error caused by the nonlinear relationship between the polarization internal resistance and the discharge current. so the optimized JEVS test is used to accurately estimate the 10s peak power of different SOC under 25â„ƒ. Fig. 6. Constant current charge/discharge, optimized JEVS: (a) Peak current of diffenrent temperature (50%SOC), (b) Peak current of different duration (25â„ƒ,50%SOC) 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 In order to verify whether the optimized JEVS test is also suitable for battery power testing at high and low temperatures, we need to compare the optimized JEVS test with the constant current charge/discharge test at different temperatures. The test peak current in 10â„ƒ, 25â„ƒ and 45â„ƒ (50%SOC) is shown in Fig.6(a), the results show that the error of the peak current estimated by JEVS is still less than 10% in 10â„ƒ, 25â„ƒ and 45â„ƒ (50%SOC), so the optimized JEVS test is suitable for estimating the 10s peak power at different temperatures. Battery electric vehicles generally select 30s as the test pulse width, the peak power duration of the Hybrid Vehicles will not exceed 60s, so BMSs are required to provide real-time peak input power and peak output power with different durations in actual operation of electric vehicles. Therefore, it is necessary to compare constant current charge/discharge test with optimized JEVS test at different time scales, the test results are shown in Fig.6(b). The results show that the error of optimized JEVS test is still within 10% under different time scales in 25â„ƒ (SOC=0.5). Based on the above analysis, under the premise that the error is less than 10%, the optimized JEVS test can efficiently estimate the battery peak power at different temperatures, different SOC and different time scales. 4. Conclusion In this study, an optimized JEVS power test is presented, and three power testing methods are compared, the constant current charge and discharge test results are used as benchmark data. The optimized JEVS test can effectively estimate the battery peak current at different SOC compared with the HPPC test in 25â„ƒ . At the same time, in order to meet the actual operation demand of electric vehicles, comparative analysis of different temperatures and time scales are introduced. The results show that the optimized JEVS test can estimate the battery peak power at different temperatures, different SOC and different time scales accurately and quickly. The conclusion is important for preventing battery overcharge or overdischarge and improving battery safety. Furthermore, the optimized JEVS method can improve the estimation accuracy of peak power, which also provide the basis for improving the battery utilization and reducing the operating cost of vehicles. Acknowledgements This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China (Grant Number 2017YFB1201005). References [1] B.O. Varga, Electric vehicles, primary energy sources and CO2 emissions:Romanian case study, Energy 49 (2013) 51â€“72. [2] L. Saw, Y. Ye, A. Tay, Electro-thermal analysis and integration issues of lithiumion battery for electric vehicles, Appl. Energy 131 (2014) 97-107. [3] Lu L, Han X, Li J, Hua J, Ouyang M. A review on the key issues for lithium-ion battery management in electric vehicles. J Power Sources 2013;226:272-88. [4] Giuliano MR, Advani SG, Prasad AK. Thermal analysis and management of lithiumetitanate batteries. J Power Sources 2011;196(15):6517- 24 [5] J. C. Jiang, C. P. Zhang, The prediction of battery pack peak power, Fundamentals and Applications of Lithium-ion Batteries in Electric Drive Vehicles, John Wiley & Sons Singapore Pte. Ltd, Singapore. 2015, pp. 103â€“112. [6] Li Fang, Yang Yifu, Yang Lihua. HEV study the output power of the battery using[J]. power technology, 2009 (3):210-212 [7] Cui Jintao, Ceng Qingdong, Wang Tianli. Study on the peak power test method of lead-acid battery[J]. battery, 2016 (1): 15-17 [8] Idaho National Engineering &Environmental Laboratory ( INEEL). Freedom CAR Battery Test Manual for Power-Assist Hybrid Electric Vehicles. DOE/ID-11069, 2003, PDF version. [9] Japan electric vehicle society. Test method of input and out put power density of nickel-hydride battery for Hybrid Electric vehicles[S]. Japan: 2003. [10] Wu-Jie, Jiang, Ning, Zhang, Peng-Cheng, Li, Nan, Chen. A Temperature-Based Peak Power Capability Estimation Method for Lithium-Ion Batteries[J]. Procedia Engineering, 2017, (187): 249-256 ration (25â„ƒ,50%SOC) 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 In order to verify whether the optimized JEVS test is also suitable for battery power testing at high and low temperatures, we need to compare the optimized JEVS test with the constant current charge/discharge test at different temperatures. The test peak current in 10â„ƒ, 25â„ƒ and 45â„ƒ (50%SOC) is shown in Fig.6(a), the results show that the error of the peak current estimated by JEVS is still less than 10% in 10â„ƒ, 25â„ƒ and 45â„ƒ (50%SOC), so the optimized JEVS test is suitable for estimating the 10s peak power at different temperatures. Battery electric vehicles generally select 30s as the test pulse width, the peak power duration of the Hybrid Vehicles will not exceed 60s, so BMSs are required to provide real-time peak input power and peak output power with different durations in actual operation of electric vehicles. Therefore, it is necessary to compare constant current charge/discharge test with optimized JEVS test at different time scales, the test results are shown in Fig.6(b). The results show that the error of optimized JEVS test is still within 10% under different time scales in 25â„ƒ (SOC=0.5). Based on the above analysis, under the premise that the error is less than 10%, the optimized JEVS test can efficiently estimate the battery peak power at different temperatures, different SOC and different time scales. 4. Conclusion In this study, an optimized JEVS power test is presented, and three power testing methods are compared, the constant current charge and discharge test results are used as benchmark data. The optimized JEVS test can effectively estimate the battery peak current at different SOC compared with the HPPC test in 25â„ƒ . At the same time, in order to meet the actual operation demand of electric vehicles, comparative analysis of different temperatures and time scales are introduced. The results show that the optimized JEVS test can estimate the battery peak power at

head.ce:title: Research on peak power test method for Lithium Ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Active Distribution Network Technology Research Center, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing, 100044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Active Distribution Network Technology Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CRRC Insdustrial Institute Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hao Hua Engineering Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100143, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The peak power of the battery (SOP) is an important parameter index for electric vehicle to improve the efficiency of battery utilization and ensure the safety of the system in the maximum limit. The estimation and prediction of SOP is based on a large number of test data at different temperature, different SOC and different time scales. It is important to improve SOP test efficiency under the premise of guaranteeing accuracy, which is also significant for BMS algorithm design and development investment reduction. In this paper, with 2.75Ah ternary Li-ion battery as the research object, the test efficiency and accuracy of the current peak power test methods (HPPC, JEVS and constant current charge and discharge ) are compared at different temperatures, through optimizing the JEVS, the test results meet the requirements at different time scales on the basis of high efficiency test, and the optimized JEVS method is verified. The experimental results indicate that the optimized JEVS can accurately estimate the peak power of the battery at different temperatures and greatly shorten the test time.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electric vehicles, primary energy sources and CO2 emissions:Romanian case study
Electro-thermal analysis and integration issues of lithiumion battery for electric vehicles, Appl
A review on the key issues for lithium-ion battery management in electric vehicles
Thermal analysis and management of lithiumetitanate batteries
The prediction of battery pack peak power, Fundamentals and Applications of Lithium-ion Batteries in Electric Drive Vehicles,

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Temperature-Based Peak Power Capability Estimation Method for Lithium-Ion Batteries[J]
